index_randomcollection_text: RandomCollection offers curated articles about economics, finance, statistics, and coding.
index_why_text: The idea of creating RandomCollection emerged when I was thinking about a way to collect and organise small private coding projects. In addition, I was looking for a way to centrally preserve random notes about various topics that would otherwise get lost in the depth of my computer. The requirement of having this random collection available at all times, device-independent, and without the need of any login, combined with my general interest in building a website, lead to the creation of RandomCollection.
index_who_text: I am an analytics and modelling risk manager with more than seven years of experience in quantitative credit risk modelling working for a Fortune Global 500 company in the financial services industry. As a trained economist and econometrician with a strong quantitative background, I have a passion for providing actionable insights of the highest quality for a wide range of business problems within the credit risk context. I utilise state-of-the-art analytics and technology to enable sound data-driven decision making for senior management and executives. It was only in my current role that I really got my hands into coding, and I especially fell in love with Python.
index_tsp_text: Above is a Travelling Salesperson Problem (TSP) rendered image of myself. More information can be found via Travelling-Salesperson-Problem-Art.
economics_footnote_1: <sup>1</sup> A thing is worth only what someone else will pay for it.
credit_risk_modelling_text: Credit Risk Modelling is a Python library providing a curated selection of modules to perform credit risk modelling analytics.
coding_git_text: Git is a software. In particular, Git is an open source, distributed version control system. In contrast, GitHub is a service. In particular, GitHub is a centralised source code hosting platform for collaborating on Git repositories.

items.text: git add <file>: add <file> to the staging area
git add .: add all files to the staging area
git add -all: add all changes to the staging area including, for example, the deletion of files
git branch: show local branches
git branch new <branch>: create a new branch <branch>
git branch -a: show all local branches
git branch -D <branch>: delete branch <branch>
git branch -m <name_old> <name_new>: change the name of branch <name_old> from <name_old> to <name_new>
git branch -r: show remote branches
git checkout <branch>: switch to branch <branch>
git checkout <file>: undo changes to the unstaged file <file>, i.e. pull the file <file> back to the staging area
git checkout .: undo changes to all unstaged files
git checkout -b <branch>: create a new branch <branch>
git clone <name_of_GitHub_repository>.git: clone repository to the local machine
git commit -m '<message>': commit changes with message '<message>'
git config --global user.email 'johndoe@google.com': set user mail
git config --global user.name 'John Doe': set user name
git init: initialise Git (creates a hidden '.git' file)
git log: show the log (enter 'q' to quit)
git merge <branch>: merge current branch with branch <branch>
git merge <source> <destination>: merge from <source> to <destination>
git mv <name_file> <name_folder>: move file <name_file> to <name_folder>
git mv <name_old> <name_new>: rename folder <name_old> from <name_old> to <name_new>
git rm -r <folder>: delete folder <folder>
git stash: temporarily save files, for example, to move from one branch to another
git status: show the status
git --version: show the Git version

coding_git_other_text: A .gitignore file specifies intentionally untracked files that Git should ignore.
coding_html_text_1: RandomCollection is hosted via GitHub Pages. The corresponding GithHub repository can be found here. In general, modern web design relies on three building blocks:

items.text: Structure: Hypertext Markup Language (HTML)
Style: Cascading Style Sheets (CSS)
Interactivity: JavaScript

coding_html_text_2: The usage of JavaScript for RandomCollection is very limited for the website is static in nature. The usage of responsive web design is limited as well and a correct appearance of the website across different devices cannot be guaranteed.

items.text: cd <directory>: change the current directory to <directory>
cd ~: change to home directory
cd /: change to root directory
cd ..: change to parent directory
clear: clear the terminal screen
cp <from> <to>: copy files or directories from <from> to <to>
echo '<text>': display <text>
head <file>: display the first few lines of <file>
kill <id>: terminate a process by its ID <id>
ls: list files and directories
ls -a: list all
ls -l: list in long format showing permissions
ls -l: list in long format including hidden files
mkdir <directory>: create a new directory <directory>
mv <from> <to>: move or rename files and directories from <from> to <to>
ps: display the list of running processes
pwd: display the current directory path
rm <file/directory>: remove file <file> or directory <directory>
rm -r <file/directory>: recursively remove file <file> or directory <directory>

coding_sas_text: The Statistical Analysis System (SAS) is a statistical software suite developed by the SAS Institute.
coding_sql_text: The Structured Query Language (SQL) is the underlying programming language for Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS) such as IBM Db2, Microsoft SQL Server, MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQLite. Typically, every RDBMS has its own slightly different SQL dialect. Transact-SQL or T-SQL, for example, is the SQL dialect in case of the Microsoft SQL Server, while PL/Pg SQL is the SQL dialect used by PostgreSQL. The following is a random collection of SQL related topics.

coding_sql_cte_text: A Common Table Expression (CTE) is a powerful construct in SQL that helps simplify a query. This reduction in complexity is achieved by deconstructing ordinarily complex queries into simple blocks to be used, and reused if necessary, in rewriting the query. Much like a derived table, the result of a CTE is not stored and exists only for the duration of the query.
